sections.title: Useful notes
items: When the **if-clause** comes first, we put a comma before the result clause.
✓ **If the weather improves, we'll go hiking.**
When the result clause comes first, a comma is not usually needed.
✓ **We'll stay inside if it rains later.**

sections.title: Use
rows: To talk about general truths, habits or scientific facts

sections.title: Use
rows: To talk about real or likely future situations
If we **leave** now, we **'ll catch** the train.

sections.title: Useful notes
items: **If you practise more, you might improve quickly.**
**If you're tired, go to bed earlier!**

sections.title: Use
rows: To talk about imaginary or unlikely situations
If I **won** the lottery, I **would travel** around the world.

sections.title: Useful notes
items: **If I were you, I'd apologise immediately.**
**If I was you, I'd take a break.**

exercises.title: A: Zero conditional
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct present simple form.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with the correct present simple form.

items.sentence: If you ...... (freeze) water, it ...... (become) ice.
If children ...... (not sleep) enough, they ...... (feel) tired.
If people ...... (exercise) regularly, they usually ...... (stay) healthy.
If I ...... (eat) too late, I ...... (have) stomach problems.
If we ...... (combine) red and white, we ...... (make) pink.
If students ...... (not listen), they ...... (miss) important information.

exercises.title: B: First conditional
exercises.instructions: Complete the first conditional sentences with the correct verb forms.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the first conditional sentences with the correct verb forms.

items.sentence: If the weather ...... (improve), we ...... (go) to the beach.
If you ...... (practise) every day, you ...... (improve) quickly.
If I ...... (meet) Jake later, I ...... (invite) him to dinner.
If they ...... (catch) the early train, they ...... (arrive) before noon.
If we ...... (not leave) soon, we ...... (be) late.
If you ...... (feel) sick tomorrow, ...... (stay) at home.

exercises.title: C: Second conditional
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct second conditional form.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with the correct second conditional form.

items.sentence: If I ...... (know) another language, I ...... (work) abroad.
If she ...... (have) more confidence, she ...... (speak) in public more often.
If we ...... (own) a bigger house, we ...... (invite) more guests.
If he ...... (not waste) so much time online, he ...... (finish) earlier.
If they ...... (live) closer, we ...... (see) them every weekend.
If I ...... (be) you, I ...... (accept) the offer.

exercises.title: D: Choose the correct conditional
exercises.instructions: Circle the correct option in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap the correct option in each sentence.

items.originalSentence: If you **touch / touched** fire, it **burns / would burn**.
items.completedSentence: If you touch, burns fire, it **burns / would burn**.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "touch, burns" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "touch, burns" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "touch" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.touch: "touch" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("fire, it **burns / would burn**.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: If she **works / worked** harder, she **will succeed / succeeds**.
items.completedSentence: If she works, will succeed harder, she **will succeed / succeeds**.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "works, will succeed" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "works, will succeed" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "works" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.works: "works" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("harder, she **will succeed / succeeds**.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: If I **am / were** famous, I **would help / help** charities.
items.completedSentence: If I were, would help famous, I **would help / help** charities.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "were, would help" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "were, would help" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "am" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.am: "am" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("famous, I **would help / help** charities.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: the battery, the phone **dies / will die**.
items.originalSentence: If we **don't charge / didn't charge** the battery, the phone **dies / will die**.
items.completedSentence: If we don't charge, will die the battery, the phone **dies / will die**.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "don't charge, will die" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "don't charge, will die" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "don't charge" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.don't charge: "don't charge" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("the battery, the phone **dies / will die**.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: a taxi now, they **will arrive / arrive** earlier.
items.originalSentence: If they **take / took** a taxi now, they **will arrive / arrive** earlier.
items.completedSentence: If they take, will arrive a taxi now, they **will arrive / arrive** earlier.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "take, will arrive" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "take, will arrive" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "take" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.take: "take" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("a taxi now, they **will arrive / arrive** earlier.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: more organised, he **wouldn't forget / doesn't forget** deadlines.
items.originalSentence: If he **was / were** more organised, he **wouldn't forget / doesn't forget** deadlines.
items.completedSentence: If he were, wouldn't forget more organised, he **wouldn't forget / doesn't forget** deadlines.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "were, wouldn't forget" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "were, wouldn't forget" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "was" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.was: "was" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("more organised, he **wouldn't forget / doesn't forget**…").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: E: Correct the errors
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has an error in a conditional structure. Write the whole sentence correctly.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has an error in a conditional structure. Write the whole sentence correctly.

items.displayPrompt: If I would know the answer, I would tell you.

cues: If I would know the answer, I would tell you.

items.answer: If I knew the answer, I would tell you.
acceptedAnswers: If I knew the answer, I would tell you.

items.displayPrompt: If he will leave now, he will catch the bus.

cues: If he will leave now, he will catch the bus.

items.answer: If he leaves now, he will catch the bus.
acceptedAnswers: If he leaves now, he will catch the bus.

items.displayPrompt: Plants die if they won't get water.

cues: Plants die if they won't get water.

items.answer: Plants die if they don't get water.
acceptedAnswers: Plants die if they don't get water.

items.displayPrompt: If you would practise more, you would improve quickly.

cues: If you would practise more, you would improve quickly.

items.answer: If you practised more, you would improve quickly.
acceptedAnswers: If you practised more, you would improve quickly.

items.displayPrompt: If I were you, I wouldn't stayed there.

cues: If I were you, I wouldn't stayed there.

items.answer: If I were you, I wouldn't stay there.
acceptedAnswers: If I were you, I wouldn't stay there.

items.displayPrompt: Unless she will hurry, she'll miss the lesson.

cues: Unless she will hurry, she'll miss the lesson.

items.answer: Unless she hurries, she'll miss the lesson.
acceptedAnswers: Unless she hurries, she'll miss the lesson.

exercises.title: F: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence:
Metal expands when it gets hot.
...... metal, it expands.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is zero conditional rewrite, so the correct answer is "If you heat".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "If you heat", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conditional sentences pair specific verb forms in the if-clause and main clause.

items.sentence:
Maybe we won't arrive before dark.
We ...... before dark.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is possibility, so the correct answer is "might not arrive".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "might not arrive", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
She never practises, so she doesn't improve.
If she ......, she would improve.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is second conditional, so the correct answer is "practised more".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
He isn't fit because he never exercises.
If he exercised regularly, he ...... fitter.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is second conditional, so the correct answer is "would be".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
I don't own a car, so I walk everywhere.
If I ...... a car, I wouldn't walk everywhere.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is second conditional, so the correct answer is "had".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
People become stressed when they work too much.
...... too much, people become stressed.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is zero conditional, so the correct answer is "If people work".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "If people work", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conditional sentences pair specific verb forms in the if-clause and main clause.
